title: Today's AI Brief: South Africa Has AI Leverage. Its Draft Policy Leaves It Unused
summary: A look at South Africa's AI policy and Google I/O 2026 Dialogues stage highlights
imageAlt: Image from source article for South Africa Has AI Leverage. Its Draft Policy Leaves It Unused
imageCaption: South Africa Has AI Leverage. Its Draft Policy Leaves It Unused
imageSourceTitle: South Africa Has AI Leverage. Its Draft Policy Leaves It Unused

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI brief. Today, I'm looking at two interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First, South Africa is making waves with its draft AI policy, or rather, the lack thereof. According to a report from IEEE Spectrum, South Africa has a unique opportunity to leverage AI, but its current draft policy leaves much to be desired. I was surprised to learn that South Africa is being looked at as a leader in AI governance, especially considering the country's unique challenges and opportunities.

The article highlights that South Africa's draft policy has been criticized for being too vague, leaving the country unsure of how to proceed. This is interesting because it shows that even countries with a lot of potential for AI growth are struggling to create effective policies. Meanwhile, Google's Dialogues stage at I/O 2026 brought together some big names to discuss the latest technological breakthroughs. What stands out to me is the emphasis on AI's role in shaping society and our future. It's clear that AI is no longer just a tech topic, but a societal one.

I also caught a glimpse of some of the other discussions happening at the Dialogues stage, and it's clear that there's a lot of excitement around AI's potential to drive positive change. From healthcare to education, AI is being explored as a tool to make a real impact on people's lives. What's more, the conversations are becoming more nuanced, with a focus on the ethics and implications of AI development.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
